cards.back: A deadlock is a situation where two or more processes are unable to proceed because each is waiting for the other to release a resource.

There are two types of deadlocks: Logical Deadlock and Physical Deadlock. Logical Deadlock occurs when two or more processes request a resource that is being held by other processes but are not in a physical location to obtain it. Physical Deadlock occurs when the processes are in the same location but unable to obtain a resource.

For a deadlock to occur, the following four conditions must be met: Mutual Exclusion (i.e., only one process can access a resource at a time), Hold and Wait (i.e., a process is holding a resource and waiting for another), No Preemption (i.e., the operating system does not preempt one process to give the resource to another), and Circular Wait (i.e., each process is waiting for a resource held by another process).
There are several algorithms for detecting deadlocks, including the Banker's Algorithm, Dining Philosophers Algorithm, and the Wait-For Algorithm.
There are several algorithms for preventing deadlocks, including the Resource Allocation Algorithm, the Priority Algorithm, and the Time Slice Algorithm.
